name: Chenowith Creek
short_description: Public BLM rockhounding along scenic Chenowith Creek in central Oregon. Hunt for agate, chalcedony, and jasper specimens in creek beds and surrounding slopes with no permit required.
description:
Chenowith Creek offers accessible rockhounding on public BLM land near Chenoweth in Wasco County. This area sits within the John Day Fossil Beds geological region where ancient volcanic activity and hydrothermalization created abundant silica-rich mineral deposits. The creek and surrounding gentle slopes expose quality agate nodules, chalcedony pieces, and jasper specimens that can be collected for personal use.

The terrain features a gentle slope along the creek valley with exposure to light-colored volcanic rhyolite and basalt formations. Summer water flow through the creek makes access easier and reveals fresh material in gravel bars. The landscape transitions from open creek bed to sagebrush-covered hillsides, providing variety in where you can search.

This is an excellent beginner-friendly location since it requires no permits, no fees, and no special equipment. The relatively flat terrain near the creek makes it accessible for families and casual rockhounders. Most collectors focus on the active creek bed where seasonal movement exposes new material, though hillside collecting is also productive.

Water can be seasonal, so spring and early summer provide the best conditions. The area sees moderate use from local rockhounders but rarely feels crowded. Always respect the 25-pound daily limit and pack out what you pack in to help preserve access for future visitors.
history: Chenowith Creek lies in the Rain Shadow region of central Oregon, where the Cascade Range blocks Pacific moisture and creates a semi-arid environment. The geological formations here are dominated by Miocene-age volcanic rocks from the Columbia River Basalt Group and John Day Formation, which underwent localized silification and mineral enrichment through hydrothermal fluids. These processes created the secondary deposits of agate and chalcedony that rockhounders find today in creek beds and exposed slopes.
tips: Search the active creek bed during spring and early summer when water flow exposes fresh gravel and reveals gemmy pieces in light-colored pebbles. Focus on inside bends of the creek where heavier agates and chalcedony settle, and check the bases of small erosional features where stones accumulate. Bring a 5-gallon bucket and 10x hand lens to examine specimens in place before collecting, and wear sturdy hiking boots since creek crossings may be necessary. Look for translucent or banded specimens with good color - local agate here often shows white, gray, and tan banding typical of Miocene silica deposits.
terrain_notes: Gentle slope terrain along Chenowith Creek valley with sagebrush and scattered juniper vegetation. Elevation approximately 1,800-2,000 feet. Creek access requires short hikes of 0.25 to 1 mile from parking areas. Footing varies from smooth gravel bars near the creek to rocky hillside slopes. Early and mid-season water flow can make creek crossings necessary; bring good footwear for wet conditions. Open sagebrush areas offer full sun exposure with little shade.
best_season: April - October. Spring and early summer (April-June) provide creek flow that exposes fresh material in gravel beds. Late summer and fall offer excellent dry conditions for hillside searching. Winter can bring intermittent snow and rain to the region, and creek access becomes more difficult.
written_directions: From Chenoweth, drive east on Highway 197 toward Tygh Valley. After approximately 3-4 miles, look for Forest Service roads or unmarked pullouts on the north side leading toward Chenowith Creek drainage. The creek is accessible from several informal parking areas along backroads in the vicinity. GPS coordinates (45.621406, -121.248334) mark a central access point. Local rockhounding guides or inquiries at a nearby rock shop can provide specific turn-by-turn details since access roads are informal.
road_conditions: Unpaved Forest Service and BLM roads with light washboarding. Passenger vehicles can typically reach parking areas, though high-clearance preferred during wet seasons. Road conditions vary - check locally before visiting.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and parking areas along BLM access roads. No developed parking lot. Space available for 3-5 vehicles at typical access points. Arrive early during peak season to secure parking.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 pounds per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Pack out all trash. Respect any seasonal closures or habitat protection areas. Do not collect from marked archaeological or paleontological sites.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No mechanical digging or ground disturbance
Pack out all trash
Respect closure signs and protected areas
nearest_services: Tygh Valley, approximately 8-10 miles south, has limited services. The Dalles, approximately 20 miles north, offers full services including gas, food, water, and supplies.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain with gentle slopes accessible to most hikers. Unpaved parking areas with minimal development. Creek bed walking is easy with stable footing on most gravel bars. Hillside searching involves slightly uneven rocky terrain. Short walking distances (under 1 mile) from parking to productive areas make this suitable for families and those with limited mobility. No wheelchair access to creek bed areas.

faq.question: What gemstones can you find rockhounding at Chenowith Creek?
faq.answer: Chenowith Creek is known for agate, chalcedony, jasper, and quartz specimens. The agates found here are typically banded in white, gray, and tan colors and often show translucency. Chalcedony pieces vary from opaque to semi-translucent, and jasper specimens range from pale to rich earth tones. Most collectors focus on the 0.5 to 3-inch size range, though larger specimens are occasionally found.

faq.question: Do you need a permit to rockhound at Chenowith Creek in Oregon?
faq.answer: No permit is required for rockhounding at Chenowith Creek. The area is public BLM land open to personal use collection under Oregon recreational rockhounding guidelines. You must follow the 25-pound daily limit per FLPMA and collect for personal use only. Commercial collection and motorized excavation are not permitted.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound near Chenoweth Oregon?
faq.answer: The best rockhounding season at Chenowith Creek runs from April through October. Spring and early summer (April-June) are ideal because creek water flow exposes fresh material in gravel bars, making it easier to spot specimens. Fall offers excellent dry conditions for hillside searching. Winter weather makes access more difficult and less productive.

faq.question: Is Chenowith Creek a good rockhounding spot for beginners?
faq.answer: Yes, Chenowith Creek is excellent for beginners. No permits or fees are required, the terrain is gentle and accessible with short walks from parking areas, and the creek bed yields attractive specimens relatively easily. Family-friendly access and the simple task of searching creek beds for visible agate and chalcedony make this ideal for learning rockhounding fundamentals without specialized skills or equipment.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do you need for rockhounding at Chenowith Creek?
faq.answer: Basic rockhounding at Chenowith Creek requires sturdy hiking boots for creek crossings, a 5-gallon bucket for carrying specimens, a 10x hand lens for examining stones in place, and a small geology hammer if you want to test hardness. A backpack for water and snacks is essential. No motorized tools or heavy excavation equipment is permitted on BLM land. Most collectors use only their hands and simple tools to extract material from loose creek beds.

faq.question: How far is Chenowith Creek from town and what services are nearby?
faq.answer: Chenowith Creek is approximately 9 miles south of Tygh Valley and 20 miles south of The Dalles. Tygh Valley offers limited services, while The Dalles has full amenities including gas stations, restaurants, water, and rockhounding supply shops. Plan to bring water and snacks for the day, as the rockhounding area itself has no facilities. Check current conditions locally before visiting, especially during wet seasons.

meta_description: Hunt agate, chalcedony & jasper at Chenowith Creek on public BLM land near Chenoweth, Oregon. No permits, beginner-friendly, best April-October.
